title: Better Real Estate ETF: Vanguard's VNQI vs. iShares' ICF
author: Robert Izquierdo
description: Vanguard's VNQI and iShares' ICF offer different real estate investment strategies. VNQI provides global diversification across 682 holdings with a lower 0.12% expense ratio and 4.3% dividend yield, while ICF focuses on 30 large U.S. REITs with a higher 0.32% expense ratio and 2.6% yield. VNQI delivered 18.2% returns over one year versus ICF's 8.9%, making it attractive for income-focused investors seeking international exposure, though it carries higher currency and foreign market risks.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VNQI demonstrates superior performance with 18.2% 1-year returns, lower expense ratio (0.12%), higher dividend yield (4.3%), and broader diversification across 682 holdings in 30+ countries, making it more attractive for cost-conscious, income-focused investors.

ICF offers a focused U.S. real estate strategy with lower volatility and reduced drawdown risk, but is disadvantaged by higher expense ratio (0.32%), lower dividend yield (2.6%), weaker 1-year returns (8.9%), and limited diversification with only 30 holdings.
Mentioned as a major holding in ICF portfolio; no specific performance commentary provided in the article.

title: These 2 Asset Classes Had the Best February. Can It Continue?
author: Matthew Benjamin
description: International developed market stocks and REITs outperformed U.S. equities in February 2026, with VEA rising 6.1% and REITs gaining 5-5.8%, while the S&P 500 fell 0.9% and Nasdaq-100 dropped 2.3%. The rotation away from mega-cap tech stocks toward international markets and real estate is expected to continue due to lower interest rate prospects, better valuations abroad, and investor diversification away from U.S. stocks.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Best performing asset class in February with 6.1% monthly gain and 7.5% year-to-date return, benefiting from healthy economic growth, fiscal stimulus, and investor rotation away from expensive U.S. stocks.
U.S. REITs rose 5.4% in February and are expected to continue outperforming due to anticipated lower interest rates, which benefit debt-heavy real estate investments.
Foreign REITs gained 5.8% in February and are positioned to continue outperforming alongside the broader trend of international diversification and lower interest rate expectations.

title: Investing in Real Estate? VNQI Goes Global While GQRE Focuses on Quality.
author: Sarah Sidlow
description: VNQI and GQRE are two real estate ETFs with different strategies: VNQI offers broader international diversification across 700+ holdings with lower fees (0.12% expense ratio) and higher dividend yield, while GQRE focuses on quality global REITs with 170 holdings and has outperformed VNQI over five years despite higher fees (0.45%). VNQI delivered stronger one-year returns (15.9% vs 3.6%), though global real estate is expected to outperform U.S. real estate for the first time since 2017.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VNQI demonstrates strong performance with 15.9% one-year return, lower expense ratio (0.12%), higher dividend yield (4.27%), larger AUM ($3.9B) providing stability, and broader international diversification across 700+ holdings in 30+ countries. Lower volatility (beta 0.88) appeals to risk-conscious investors.

GQRE shows superior five-year performance with $1,043 growth vs VNQI's $851.21 on $1,000 invested, demonstrating the effectiveness of its quality-focused strategy. However, higher expense ratio (0.45%), lower one-year return (3.6%), and smaller AUM ($359.7M) are drawbacks compared to VNQI.
Top holding in GQRE (1.02%), representing quality U.S. REIT exposure within the fund's quality-focused strategy.
Second-largest holding in GQRE (+3.67%), representing quality data center REIT exposure within the fund's portfolio.

title: VNQI vs RWX: Broad International Property Exposure or Regional Concentration
author: Eric Trie
description: VNQI and RWX are international real estate ETFs with different approaches: VNQI offers broader diversification across 682 holdings with lower fees (0.12% expense ratio) and higher dividend yield (4.27%), while RWX concentrates on 119 holdings with higher fees (0.59%) but delivered stronger 1-year returns (21.8% vs 15.9%). The choice depends on investor preference for diversification versus regional concentration.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VNQI is presented as the superior choice for cost-conscious, diversification-focused investors with a significantly lower expense ratio (0.12% vs 0.59%), higher dividend yield (4.27%), larger AUM ($3.9B), and broader portfolio of 682 holdings that reduce concentration risk.

RWX is presented as a viable alternative for investors comfortable with regional concentration and willing to accept higher fees for potentially stronger short-term returns. While it delivered better 1-year performance (21.8%), it has higher costs and less diversification, making it suitable only for specific investor profiles.

title: Cypress Point Ups Its Global Real Estate Exposure
author: Emma Newbery
description: Cypress Point Wealth Management purchased 65,258 shares of Vanguard Global ex-U.S. Real Estate ETF, increasing its international real estate investment to $6.05 million, representing 1.2% of its assets under management.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Provides broad international real estate market exposure with a 4.3% dividend yield and passive indexing strategy
Remains the largest holding in Cypress Point's portfolio at 44.9% of assets under management
